title: Chapter 2 Quiz: Pathfinder
description: Test your knowledge of the Linux filesystem and navigation commands.

questions.question: What is the top-level directory in Linux called?
questions.explanation: The root directory is represented by a single forward slash `/`.

questions.question: The `cd` command with no arguments takes you to the root directory.
questions.explanation: False. `cd` with no arguments takes you to your Home directory.

questions.question: Which command shows the full path of your current working directory?
questions.explanation: `pwd` stands for Print Working Directory.

questions.question: You want to see the file size in 'human-readable' format (e.g., 4.0K). Which option do you use with `ls`?
questions.explanation: The `-h` flag stands for 'human-readable'.

questions.question: Which of these paths is an Absolute Path?
questions.explanation: Absolute paths always start with a `/`.

questions.question: What does `ls -a` do?
options: Shows all files, including hidden ones.
Shows files in a long list format.
questions.explanation: The `-a` flag means 'all'. It reveals files starting with a dot.

questions.question: To move up one directory level, you use the command `cd _____`.
questions.explanation: `..` represents the parent directory.

questions.question: In Linux, a file named `.config` is a hidden file.
questions.explanation: True. Any file or directory starting with a dot is hidden by default.

questions.question: What does the tilde `~` character represent in a path?
questions.explanation: `~` is a shortcut for `/home/your_username`.

questions.question: If you are in `/var/log` and run `cd ~/Downloads`, where do you end up?
questions.explanation: The `~` shortcut expands to your home directory, regardless of current location.

questions.question: Which directory contains system configuration files?
questions.explanation: `/etc` holds configuration files.

questions.question: You can delete any file in `/usr` safely as a normal user.
questions.explanation: False. `/usr` contains system software. Modifying it requires admin privileges and can break the system.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `-l` option for `ls`?
options: Show a long listing format with permissions
questions.explanation: `-l` provides a detailed list including permissions, owner, size, and date.

questions.question: What command toggles you back to the last directory you were in?
questions.explanation: `cd -` changes to the previous working directory.

questions.question: You are in `/home/user/Documents`. What is the relative path to `/home/user/Pictures`?
questions.explanation: You must go up one level (`..`) to `/home/user` and then down into `Pictures`.

questions.question: The command `ls /` will show the contents of the root directory.
questions.explanation: True. The argument `/` tells `ls` to target the root of the filesystem.

questions.question: To see hidden files and detailed information together, you would run `ls -_____`.
questions.explanation: Combining flags: `ls -l -a` can be written as `ls -la`.

questions.question: Which directory is the personal storage space for a regular user named 'bob'?
questions.explanation: User home directories are located in `/home/[username]`.

questions.question: What does the `.` (single dot) represent?
questions.explanation: `.` is a reference to the current working directory.

questions.question: Why might a hacker be interested in the `/var/log` directory?
options: It contains system and application logs.
It contains temporary files that are deleted on reboot.
questions.explanation: `/var/log` stores log files which can reveal what commands were run and what connections were made to the system.
